name: Vannevar Bush — Memex
organization: Office of Scientific Research and Development
short_description: A 1945 thought experiment describing a desk-sized personal memory machine that stores all of a person's records and lets them traverse information via associative trails.
long_description: Published in The Atlantic in July 1945, Vannevar Bush's essay 'As We May Think' introduced the Memex (memory extender) — a hypothetical desk-sized electromechanical device using compressed microfilm to store all the books, records, and communications a person might ever consult. Its defining innovation was associative linking: rather than rigid index hierarchies, the user could forge 'trails' connecting any two items and share those trails with others, mimicking the way human minds move by association rather than by category. The Memex was never built, but it is the foundational thought experiment for hypertext (Ted Nelson, 1965), personal computing, lifelogging, and every AI memory system that followed. Douglas Engelbart cited it directly in designing the mouse and NLS; Tim Berners-Lee acknowledged it as an ancestor of the web; Microsoft's MyLifeBits (2003) and DARPA's Memex program (2014) took its name explicitly.
ai_note: No AI/ML exists in the concept — it was conceived as an electromechanical microfilm device. The intelligence is human; the machine extends memory and enables associative retrieval mechanically. AI is part of what the vision ultimately became, not what Bush specified.

interactions.title: Call up a book by tapping a code
interactions.description: The owner taps a few keys to summon any book, record, or letter from the microfilm store, and it appears projected on one of the desk's slanting translucent screens for reading.

interactions.title: Drop a new page onto the platen
interactions.description: To add fresh material, the user lays a letter or photograph on the platen and depresses a lever; a dry-photography process copies it onto the next blank frame of film.

interactions.title: Tie two items together into a trail
interactions.description: Reading two related pages side by side, the user joins them with a code so that tapping a button later jumps instantly from one to the other — and can branch a side trail off to a particular item.

interactions.title: Build the Turkish-bow trail
interactions.description: Bush's worked example: curious why the Turkish bow bested the English longbow, the owner runs through encyclopedia and history articles, branches into texts on elasticity, and threads dozens of items into one named trail with his own longhand analysis inserted.

interactions.title: Insert your own marginal note
interactions.description: Anywhere along a trail the user can add a comment of his own — either linking it into the main trail or hanging it off a side trail beside the item it speaks to.

interactions.title: Hand a trail to a friend
interactions.description: The owner reproduces an interesting trail — references plus his personal annotations — and passes the copy to a colleague, who slots it straight into his own Memex; Bush imagined 'trail blazers' who make a profession of finding such useful paths.

interactions.title: Retrace a doctor's diagnostic trail
interactions.description: A physician facing an unfamiliar case pulls up a trail another doctor blazed through prior cases, drug references, and reactions — following the associative links rather than searching a rigid index. Grounded in Bush's shareable-trail mechanism, an example he himself sketched for the medical reader.

interactions.title: Inherit a mentor's lifetime of trails
interactions.description: When a researcher retires, an apprentice loads the senior's accumulated trails into their own Memex and walks the same associative paths through a career's worth of reading — built directly on the copy-and-insert sharing the device supports.

images.caption: A modern reinterpretation of the Memex device at the Das Netz exhibition, Deutsches Technikmuseum, Berlin, 2025.
images.alt_text: A physical reconstruction of the Memex desk device on display at a museum exhibition, showing the large desk-like form factor with screens and storage components.

sources.title: As We May Think
sources.access_notes: Original essay by Vannevar Bush, published July 1945 in The Atlantic Monthly, Vol. 176, No. 1, pp. 101–108. Primary source for all claims about the Memex concept.

sources.title: Memex — Wikipedia
sources.access_notes: Secondary source documenting influence on Ted Nelson (hypertext, 1965), Douglas Engelbart (NLS), Tim Berners-Lee (WWW), Microsoft MyLifeBits (2003), and DARPA Memex program (2014).
